data.title: Hitachi: Recent Developments Support A 'Buy'
data.description:
I'm keeping Hitachi, Ltd. (HTHIY) (HTHIF) as a "Buy"-rated name. The latest news flow confirms that its financial prospects remain promising.

My prior April 3, 2025, article touched on how HTHIY will thrive on sustainability- and technology-related advancements.

Nuclear Deals Are A Catalyst

The firm aims to expand the top line for "Hitachi Energy Segment/HES" by a mid-teens CAGR between FY24 (YE Mar 2025) and FY30 to $33 billion. Analysts' projection of HTHIY's group-level annualized increase during the same timeframe is a more modest +8% (source: S&P Capital IQ).

The above-mentioned numbers send me a clear message about HES's critical role in boosting the company's future turnover.

Last month, SA News highlighted a "nuclear power project in Tennessee, where GE Vernova (GEV) and Hitachi will build BWRX-300 small modular reactors (SMRs)."

HES is currently too reliant on the Power Grids sub-segment, which contributes over nine-tenths of its operating profit. Looking ahead, it could move towards a more balanced business mix with this massive U.S. nuclear venture.

As per the recent media report, the Tennessee asset's CAPEX alone is estimated at $40 billion, or more than HES's targeted decade-end revenues.

Lifecycles Of Various Power Categories

Also, I like the fact that HTHIY has chosen SMRs over "Large Nuclear Reactors/LNRs." The former's shorter buildout period as outlined above should imply faster payback.

GEV also revealed "a memorandum of understanding with Hitachi to explore opportunities to deploy the BWRX-300 small modular reactor in Southeast Asia/ASEAN" in March.

My take is that HES's focus on this geographical region is wise.

ASEAN represents "a quarter of growth in global energy demand by 2035" based on a March 26, 2026, "Associated Press" piece. This write-up also indicated that 5 of the countries in this bloc have announced atomic-linked roadmaps.

In my opinion, the future performance of HTHIY's Energy business could surpass expectations thanks to nuclear-related tailwinds.

New Rail Win Signals Profitability Pivot

In early March, HTHIY disclosed that its subsidiary "Hitachi Rail will design and deliver the rolling stock and signaling systems" for "Turin Metro Line 2/TML2."

I think this represents a major shift from its Mobility segment's legacy asset-heavy model.

The TML2 transaction involved a bundled sale of HTHIY's higher-margin traffic management software and the comparatively lower-return passenger vehicles.

It has also cross-sold its profitable data analytics service branded as "HMAX", on top of the typical hardware sales. At its October 2025 AI strategy briefing (S&P Capital IQ transcript), management unveiled the long-term target of raising HMAX's penetration by 10x from two thousand trains currently.

Hitachi Rail has the bargaining power to charge a premium for HMAX, in my view. The latter has been able to cut clients' upkeep-related expenses by around 15 percent according to actual case studies.

The Mobility division recorded an EBITA-to-revenue of 8.9% for the most recent 3QFY2025. That's 390bps below the company-wide figure. I see this as an illustration of this business' profitability enhancement potential with deals like TML2.

Portfolio Reshaping Is In Progress

HTHIY issued a press release in late March regarding a 40-60 joint-venture with Oki Electric (OKIEY) to house their combined "Automated Teller Machines" operations.

This move is aligned with its goal to improve "Return On Invested Capital" or "ROIC" from 10.9% for FY2024 to 12%-13% by FY27.

ATMs are part of HTHIY's "Digital Systems & Services" or "DSS" segment. That was the group's most profitable business in the latest quarter, boasting an EBITA-to-revenue of 15.2%.

DSS's outperformance is mainly attributable to the Systems Integration solutions. But these margins could have been higher without the capital-intensive ATM manufacturing activities.

I anticipate that the operating profitability of DSS will be enhanced following the deconsolidation of the ATM unit. It's reasonable for me to assume that HTHIY will likely restructure other low-ROIC entities going forward.

Also, the funds freed up from such initiatives could help to boost ROICs indirectly by shrinking the capital base. HTHIY has a ¥0.1 trillion buyback authorization in effect from the end of Jan. to the end of Apr. this year. It has already completed more than half of this plan by March 31.

You Shouldn't Ignore These Downside Factors

Many nations might defer their nuclear programs in the event of any unfortunate high-profile accidents that raise safety concerns. That would hurt HES's growth outlook.

The rise in HMAX's take-up rate for the Rail operations could be slower than expected. This can be due to pricing considerations or doubts about its effectiveness.

My Closing Thoughts

I'm still bullish on this stock.

HTHIY is now trading at a forward FY26 EBITDA multiple of 12.5 times, as per S&P Capital IQ.

Siemens (SIEGY) and GEV, its listed Mobility & Energy comparables, are valued by the market at forward EV/EBITDAs of 30x and 45x, respectively.

This name is undervalued based on my peer benchmarking exercise. I expect the future growth, margin, and ROIC improvements to act as re-rating triggers.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Bank of Hawaii Stock Is Worth The Premium Valuation
data.description:
Shares of Bank of Hawaii Corporation (BOH) have been a solid performer over the past year, gaining about 24%. While the company just reported a Q1 earnings miss, investors largely shook that off, with the stock trading close to flat as adjusted earnings were more in line with expectations. The bank is enjoying meaningful tailwinds from fixed reinvestment, and this can power earnings growth for some time. While shares are a bit expensive, this premium is justified.

In the company’s first quarter, Bank of Hawaii earned $1.30, which missed expectations by $0.02 even as revenue grew 13% to $192 million. Results were weighed down by $1.3 million of investment securities losses; otherwise, the quarter was in line with expectations (these losses are generally excluded from analyst consensus). While not the fastest-growing bank, BOH has a very conservative capital structure and long-running margin tailwinds that were both made clear in the quarter.

Looking first at its balance sheet, deposits were $21 billion, down 1% sequentially, given seasonal drawdowns after the typical late Q4 build. Average deposits were up about 1% from last year. During the quarter, its cost of interest-bearing deposits declined by 22 bps, a strong pull-through of Q4 rate cuts, to 1.72%. Over the past year, it has cut deposit rates by about 44 bps. About 27% of BOH’s balances are non-interest-bearing (“NIB”). At $5.65 billion, these were up about 1.5% from last year.

The Hawaiian deposit market is a bit of an oligopoly, dominated by five players with a small presence from the national banks. Because of this, deposit pricing tends to be relatively low, enabling stronger pass-through of Fed rate cuts onto borrowers. Within this market, BOH has the leading market share. Given its already leading position, I expect long-term deposit growth to be fairly muted (closer to 2% than 4%).

On the asset side, it has $14.2 billion of loans, and average loans were flat over the past year. Relative to most regional banks, BOH has more consumer exposure with ~$8 billion of balances, where its commercial lending is just $6.2 billion. Over 80% of the consumer portfolio is tied to the housing market. With almost all of its loan portfolio secured by physical assets, loan losses tend to be low, with recoveries given default fairly high.

About 93% of its loan book is in Hawaii, so local economic factors are extremely important. As you can see below, the Hawaiian economy has meaningfully underperformed the broader U.S. over the past decade, primarily due to a much sharper decline during COVID-19 and slower subsequent recovery. While U.S. GDP returned to pre-COVID levels by early 2021, Hawaii did not do so until mid-2023. The state’s economy has much less exposure to the tech industry, a key driver of U.S. growth, and is much more dependent upon tourism. This recovery was further hampered by a devastating wildfire in 2023 and a weaker Japanese yen, as foreign tourists are a key driver of growth.

Given its reliance on tourism, I view the Hawaiian economy as having lower potential growth than the U.S. as a whole, as that is not an industry (unlike tech or manufacturing) with meaningful long-term productivity growth. That said, the economy is actually performing well compared to its potential, with unemployment of just 2.2%, roughly half the national average. For consumer credit quality, I view the level of employment as being more important than the potential growth rate—so long as people are employed, they tend to be able to make payments.

I remain cautious about the overall pace of travel spending, given broader affordability challenges in the economy. Hotel RevPAR (revenue/available room) fell in 2025 for the first time since COVID-19, and travel spending has become increasingly reliant on activity by luxury travelers in a sign of the “K-shaped” economy. Obviously, the future of the Iran War is unknowable, but I remain concerned that the market is underpricing the potential impact of the oil market disruption. If inflation stays higher for longer, that could further crimp travel spending, which is highly discretionary. As such, I expect little if any economic growth in Hawaii over the next 12 months and for unemployment to potentially tick up slightly towards 2.5%-3.0%.

BOH also has a $7.9 billion investment securities portfolio. This is a portfolio of high-quality fixed income, but like many banks, it was purchased largely when rates were lower. As such, it only yields 2.72%. With each quarter, some of the portfolio matures, and BOH can reinvest proceeds at current market yields. As a result, its portfolio yield is up 29 bps from a year ago. It will take multiple years for the portfolio to fully roll over, but this will eventually add $1.50-$2.00 of EPS, assuming a 3.5-4% long-term yield.

BOH generated $151 million of net interest income (“NII”) in Q1, up 4% sequentially. Given the fact that Q1 has two fewer calendar days, it is unusual for banks to report a sequential increase in NII, but its net interest margin (“NIM”) expanded a strong 13 bps from Q4 to 2.74%, given the significant reduction in deposit costs. This more than offset lower loan yields. NIM was up 42 bps from last year, thanks to fixed reinvestment and lower deposit costs. With ongoing fixed asset reinvestment and 0-1 Fed rate cuts, we should see BOH’s NIM approach 3% over the next year.

During the quarter, Bank of Hawaii set aside $2 million for credit losses. Net charge-offs were just $1 million, reflecting the strength of its underwriting, and BOH modestly added reserves during the quarter. It ended the quarter with $12 million in nonperforming assets, down $2 million sequentially. This was also down by $5 million from a year ago. We typically see an improvement in consumer credit quality during Q1 as tax refunds allow borrowers to catch up on past due statements.

BOH has set aside $147 million for loan losses. This covers ~1.04% of loans and is more than 10x nonperforming assets. That is an extremely strong reserve position, as I like to see coverage of 2.5x. This reserve position provides the company with a significant buffer should economic conditions worsen and delinquencies build. To a large extent, BOH has already reserved for a worse economic environment, and so earnings should be fairly well protected against a modest rise in delinquencies.

Beyond its solid reserves position, BOH maintains an excellent capital posture with a 12.1% tier 1 common equity capital (CET1) ratio. Now, given its size, BOH does not need to include the $247 million unrealized loss in AOCI within its capital calculations. Adjusting for this, BOH would still have an adjusted capital ratio of about 10.4%, which is very healthy, as I view 9.5-10% as the necessary amount of capital to safely run its business. Given its strong capital position, BOH can return capital to shareholders, including $15 million of buybacks during Q1. Shares also offer a 3.5% dividend yield.

Looking forward, I see BOH earning $5.90-$6.20 this year, assuming stable credit provisions. While I see some downside risk in the Hawaiian economy, its excess reserve position more than makes up for this. Beyond that, the bank has another $1.25+ of earnings growth over the next two years from fixed reinvestment, not to mention ~$4 of excess capital per share.

BOH shares are ~13x earnings, above my 9-12x fair value for regional banks. However, with a run-rate EPS above $7.50 over the next 18 months, shares could trade at $90 (12x run-rate earnings). BOH has an elevated multiple today because its securities portfolio provides a clear runway for earnings growth, and BOH can grow into its multiple. With ~12% upside to fair value and a 3.5% dividend, I view BOH as an attractive buy even with shares at a 52-week high.

data.title: Honeywell Q1 Preview: Portfolio Transformation In Focus, Shares Fairly Valued
data.description:
Honeywell (HON) will be releasing its Q1 this Thursday, and ahead of that release I still view shares as a ‘hold.’

I provided commentary on HON in February, and in that release, I opined that shares were fairly valued near its all-time highs. I spoke positively about HON’s portfolio transformation strategy. However, with shares already trading near its forecasted price target, I didn’t see a reason to chase shares any further.

Since my update, the stock has not changed meaningfully. It’s down about 4.5%, but that’s not exactly material enough to justify a change in stance.

An important update that warrants attention is the just announced deal regarding Brady Corporation’s (BRC) acquisition of HON’s solutions and services business. In my view, this will further support HON’s strategy priorities, though I don’t necessarily believe this is an earth-shattering announcement ahead of the Q1 release.

What Is The Outlook For HON Stock?

As I look back at the guidance provided during the Q4 call, I believe that HON laid out a fairly positive path for 2026.

HON is targeting organic sales growth of 3% to 6% and 6% to 9% earnings growth. That outlook is supported by record backlog, continued pricing discipline, and productivity actions across the automation businesses as the company prepares for the Aerospace separation in Q3.

Speaking of Aerospace, the unit is expected to grow at a high single-digit pace, while Building Automation should deliver above mid-single-digit growth. In addition, Process Automation is likely to start slower before improving in the second half as backlog converts.

In my view, all of this paints the picture of a company with steady operational momentum heading into what will likely be a transitional year for HON.

The recently announced agreement for BRC to acquire HON’s Productivity Solutions and Services (“PSS") business fits directly into this narrative. PSS generated roughly +$1.1B in sales in 2025, and I believe its divestiture further simplifies HON’s portfolio and sharpens its focus around higher-margin, less cyclical automation and software-driven businesses.

How Will The Brady Corporation Acquisition Impact Honeywell International?

The just announced sale of HON’s PSS unit to BRC is, in my view, another logical step in HON’s ongoing portfolio simplification. PSS generated approximately +$1.1B in 2025 sales and operates in mobile computers, barcode scanning, and printing solutions.

Yes, these are solid businesses, but ones that sit a bit outside of HON’s growing emphasis on higher-margin automation, software, and process technologies.

At roughly 8x EBITDA, the valuation appears reasonable and clean. In my view, HON is monetizing a non-core asset at an attractive multiple while freeing up management attention and capital ahead of the Aerospace spinoff in Q3.

This follows the same playbook we’ve seen with other recent divestitures. Streamline the portfolio, reduce complexity, and sharpen the company’s identity around Automation.

Financially, I don’t believe the deal will move the needle in a major way in the near term. Strategically, however, I believe it supports the message the management team has been delivering for over a year.

What To Watch When Honeywell Reports Q1?

With HON reporting this Thursday, the first thing I’ll be watching is whether the strong order momentum from Q4, up 23% with a record +$37B backlog, is carrying into Q1.

I believe that backlog is really the backbone of the 2026 outlook, so any commentary on conversion rates and end-market demand will matter.

A second area is Aerospace. Management has already guided to high single-digit growth for the year, but Q1 commentary around commercial OE ramp timing and defense strength will help set expectations for how smooth that trajectory actually is.

Third is Automation, particularly Building Automation and Process Automation. These are increasingly becoming the core identity of Honeywell post-spin, so any color on pricing, software adoption, and margin expansion will be important.

Finally, I expect some discussion around the BRC transaction. While it is not financially transformational, investors will likely want clarity on timing, use of proceeds, and how this further shapes the post-spin Honeywell.

More broadly, any updated detail on the Aerospace spinoff timeline and remaining portfolio actions will likely be closely watched as well. At this point, messaging around simplification matters just as much as the quarterly print itself, in my view.

Is HON Stock A Buy, Sell, Or Hold?

At the end of the day, I continue to view HON as a 'hold' heading into Q1 earnings.

For me, not much has really changed in a meaningful way since my last update. Shares are still trading near the upper end of their valuation range, not far from all-time highs, and, in my view, already reflect a lot of the positives we’ve seen over the past year.

Order trends remain strong, backlog is at record levels, and the 2026 guide points to steady mid-single-digit growth with continued margin strength.

On top of that, the portfolio transformation continues to progress, with the Aerospace spinoff still on track and additional divestitures like the just announced BRC transaction further simplifying the structure.

But that’s also part of the reason I remain neutral at current levels. In my view, investors are already paying for this cleaner, more focused HON. The strategic direction is clear, but I believe much of that clarity is already reflected in the stock’s performance and valuation.

So while I am positive on the long-term direction of the business, I don’t necessarily see a compelling near-term re-rating opportunity from here. For existing holders, I think it’s a steady name to own. For new investors, however, I would still prefer a better entry point.

data.title: Market Brief: DeFi Is Cooked? The Market Is Asking The Wrong Question
data.description:
DeFi came under fresh pressure after the KelpDAO exploit triggered a sharp shock across Aave (AAVE-USD) and revived fears around contagion, bad debt, and operational risk. Yet the fallout was not one-dimensional. Capital fled the most exposed pools, but some demand rotated elsewhere. We break down what the Aave and Drift (DRIFT-USD) episodes actually revealed, where liquidity moved next, and what this means for DeFi’s next phase.

The latest panic around Aave has revived a familiar claim that DeFi is broken. But the more useful question is whether the market is finally repricing risks it tolerated for too long.

Aave was not directly hacked, yet it still suffered a severe liquidity shock after the rsETH incident. That alone shows how DeFi’s biggest vulnerabilities now extend well beyond contract code.

Aave Was Not Hacked, but It Was Hit by Contagion

What happened to Aave matters because it was not a direct protocol breach. After attackers drained about $291 million from KelpDAO-linked infrastructure, the compromised rsETH was routed into DeFi lending rails and used to borrow large amounts of WETH.

That pushed key Aave pools into acute stress, with users struggling to withdraw and markets moving quickly to freeze rsETH exposure.

In other words, the event was less about Aave’s own code failing and more about Aave inheriting risk from an asset whose security assumptions sat outside its own control. It showed how a weakness in one part of the stack can become a balance sheet problem somewhere else.

Once confidence in a collateral asset breaks, liquidity conditions can deteriorate much faster than governance can react.

The market was not only pricing the immediate exploit. It was pricing the possibility that external assets, bridge configurations, and cross-protocol dependencies can force a major lending venue into a confidence crisis without ever touching its core contracts.

DeFi Risk Is No Longer Just Code

Aave and Drift matter for different reasons. Aave showed how risk can spread across protocols through collateral and market structure.

Drift showed how large losses can also come from operational weakness, permissions, and human error. Taken together, the message is clear: DeFi risk no longer begins and ends with contract code.

Drift itself made that point clearly. The roughly $286 million exploit exposed weaknesses in execution, permissions, and operational control.

The April attack wave made the point even clearer. In the two weeks after Drift, at least a dozen crypto entities were hit again, including Hyperbridge, Rhea Finance, and Grinex, pushing total losses for the period above $600 million.

Another warning came from Ethereum (ETH-USD). A six-month security effort backed by the Ethereum Foundation said it identified around 100 suspected DPRK-linked operatives across 53 Web3 projects.

That is why Drift should not be read as an isolated event. The bigger message is that DeFi’s threat surface now includes infiltration, identity fraud, and organizational compromise alongside contract exploits.

DeFi Flows Show Both Flight and Rotation

The flows show two things at once.

First, this was not just an Aave shock. DeFi TVL fell about 8% over 24 hours, wiping out roughly $85 billion, showing that the market’s first response was broad risk reduction across the sector. Part of the capital clearly left DeFi rather than staying inside the system.

Second, the outflows were not uniform. Aave came under clear pressure, but part of the lending demand rotated quickly into Spark. Spark’s ETH deposit rate briefly spiked to 130% and then remained unusually elevated, offering a direct signal that capital and borrow demand were shifting.

At the same time, the redistribution was only partial. Broader DeFi TVL still contracted sharply, and other major lending protocols such as Morpho and Sky (SKY-USD) also saw meaningful outflows, showing that this was not a clean one-to-one migration across the sector.

This was not only a capital flight event but also a repricing of trust. Money first left the most exposed pools, then began to separate weaker protocols from those that still retained market trust. DeFi did not see a uniform exit. It saw both deleveraging and selective reallocation.

AI and the Next Security Upgrade

AI could improve DeFi security from here. Anthropic said Claude Mythos Preview has flagged 1000+ high-severity vulnerabilities in recent weeks, including issues affecting every major operating system and web browser.

If systems with that level of capability are applied to deep DeFi audits, exploit simulation, and continuous code review, crypto security could see a meaningful upgrade.

But AI will not end the arms race. The same tools that help defenders find weaknesses faster can also lower the cost of exploit research for attackers. AI is more likely to raise the baseline of security work than to remove security risk.

For DeFi, that means better tooling alone is not enough. Stronger collateral standards, cleaner isolation, tighter operational control, and more disciplined trust assumptions still matter most. DeFi is not dying. It is being forced to mature.

Disclaimer: The information provided herein does not constitute investment advice, financial advice, trading advice, or any other sort of advice, and should not be treated as such. All content set out below is for informational purposes only.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Dow Jones And U.S. Stock Market Outlook - Back At All-Time Highs, Investors Take A Break
data.description:
By Elior Manier

US stock benchmarks extended to fresh record highs on Friday, but the wall of euphoria is officially hitting a stall as a massive geopolitical deadline looms.

The temporary US-Iran ceasefire is set to expire this Wednesday, April 22, and the market is growing increasingly anxious. Investors are desperately looking for further developments to justify current market pricing, especially following recent reports that President Trump will not extend the ceasefire—and won't open the Strait of Hormuz—unless a finalized deal is signed.

The diplomatic timeline has been frustratingly erratic.

High-stakes negotiations were initially supposed to take place last Thursday before being pushed to the weekend and now finally into this week.

JD Vance apparently still hasn't left to Islamabad to kick off the new rounds of talks, and Wall Street is now done trading on rumors only. Investors are now demanding concrete advancements before they fund the next leg of this peace trade.

Odds for a peace deal are remaining stuck below 40%, failing to extend above in recent days, and the turn from the White House rhetoric isn't helping much.

This underlying caution is already showing up in the charts. While the Nasdaq and S&P 500 recently reached stunning new records, the Dow Jones—often a much more stable guide to broad market appetite—has failed to gather the strength needed to push to all-time highs.

This divergence is a glaring signal: smart money is still highly skeptical that the global economy will seamlessly revert to its pre-war normal. That skepticism is heavily reinforced in the energy sector, where WTI crude remains uncomfortably sticky around the $90 handle.

With the clock ticking down to Wednesday and the fate of the Strait of Hormuz hanging in the balance, let's look at intraday charts and trading levels for the major US indexes: the Dow Jones Industrial Average, Nasdaq Composite, and S&P 500.

Current Session's Stock Heatmap

The market is once again sending mixed signals, with mega caps taking a hit, but the rest remaining quite resilient on the session.

The first week of earnings has been very decent, so that maintains a decent bid in stocks, but the small changes in the session can only confirm the hesitancy from recent geopolitics.

Dow Jones 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The DJIA gapped back to 49,000 at the start of the week, limited in its progress by the rebound in WTI Crude and the lack of diplomatic progress.

Now testing its gap fill at 49,560, a key test is showing up ahead:

  • Extending above the 49,760 Friday highs hints at a continuation above
  • On the other hand, a rejection of the gap fill would hint at a break of the ceasefire bull channel.
    • The 4H 50-period MA would be a decent test in such an occurrence.

Dow Jones technical levels for trading:

Resistance Levels

  • Weekend Gap fill 49,460 (testing)
  • 49,900 to 50,000 Resistance and Early 2026 Highs
  • All-Time Highs 50,544

Support Levels

  • Major Pivot – 49,000 to 49,200 (short-term bearish below)
  • Momentum Support 48,500
  • Pivotal Support at 48,000 (Mid-term Bearish below)
  • Mini Support 47,400 to 47,600
  • War Resistance now Key Support 47,000 +/- 100 Points (Bearish below)
  • January 2025 Highs 45,000 to 45,280

Nasdaq 4H Chart and Trading Levels

Nasdaq has now extended well above its prior all-time highs in a 17% firework – but the bullish momentum is coming to a stall.

The RSI Momentum is largely diverging, hinting at a stalling in the price action until markets learn more on the fundamental issue.

  • The base case is for a retest of the 26,200 prior all-time highs, a decent place of entry for optimists
  • Breaking 26,000 would hint at a larger pullback (~25,500) in the event where momentum fails to persist

Nasdaq technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Daily resistance 26,600 to 26,750
  • New all-time highs 26,736
  • Potential Resistance at 27,000

Support Levels

  • Prior ATH Pivot 26,200 to 26,300 (Short-term bearish below)
  • 25,400 to 25,500 Feb Range Intraday Support
  • War Support 25,000 to 25,250
  • 24,450 to 24,550 Key Support
  • Early 2025 ATH at 22,000 to 22,229 Support

S&P 500 4H Chart and Trading Levels

The S&P 500 is holding even better standards compared to its peers, also retesting its weekend gap fill (~7,110).

  • Rejecting it would, like Nasdaq, point to a test of its prior all-time highs (~7,000 - 7,020)
  • Rebounding from there should easily lead to new all-time highs (barring a worse fundamental outlook), as this also coincides with a test of the upward channel.

S&P 500 technical levels of interest:

Resistance Levels

  • Week-end gap 7,100 resistance (testing)
  • New all-time resistance 7,150
  • Next key potential resistance 7,200

Support Levels

  • Prior ATH Pivot 7,000 to 7,020
  • December ATH Mini support 6,945 to 6,975
  • Minor Support 6,880 to 6,900
  • Pivotal Support 6,750 to 6,770
  • 6,680 to 6,700 Key Support
  • 6,300 psychological level (War lows)

Keep track of WTI Crude and the latest headlines throughout the week to stay ahead of the curve, with investors still confused about US-Iran negotiations.

Safe trades!

Original Post

data.title: Cleveland-Cliffs Q1 Earnings: A Mundane Report At A Time Of Uncertainty
data.description:
Cleveland-Cliffs Inc.'s (CLF) slight beat on both revenue and earnings in Q1 of 2026 was not enough to impress the market, and the share price is reacting accordingly.

The market already had very low-expectations heading into the report, which is yet another reason why the drop in the early trading hours is quite concerning. To make matters worse, CLF's performance was well below those of its major peers even before the drop following today's report.

As we will see down below, the Q1 report was not bad, but the market is now getting increasingly uneasy about the macroeconomic environment and steel producers could be caught in the middle of a major cyclical swing as 2026 progresses.

Consumer sentiment, for example, was already at record-low levels in the beginning of 2026 and has now fallen even further as a consequence of recent events in the Middle East and the skyrocketing fuel prices.

Consumer sentiment sank about 11% this month, extending a decline that began with the start of the Iran conflict, and is currently about 9% below a year ago.

Source: sca.isr.umich.edu.

Higher anticipated inflation could now push consumers to rethink large purchases, such as autos, while the record-high mortgage rates could put pressure on construction.

Although the Q1 report was roughly in-line with expectations, the lack of any major positive developments has pushed even the die-hard optimists to throw-in the towel.

Overreaction Or Something Else?

It's quite easy to jump on conclusions that the sharp drop in CLF share price following the largely in-line report is an irrational move by the market. In the early trading hours the stock is already down by nearly 8%, which might seem a bit excessive and certainly not the first time for the stock to fall sharply to such a report.

In reality, however, the market is simply having a hard time pricing the stock after a period of elevated optimism in the second half of 2025 when trading volume skyrocketed and CLF saw a major multiple repricing.

The issue is that following the Q2 2025 report and the consequent Q3 report, it seemed as if CLF was headed straight towards achieving gross profitability in the coming quarters. The Q4 2025 report was quite disappointing in that regard, and today's release still included a negative gross profit figure.

Many investors were likely front-running the anticipated recovery following the tariffs, which have already lead to a collapse of steel imports. But as we saw above CLF is still struggling to achieve gross profitability and a potential deterioration of the macroeconomic conditions in the second half of 2026 could push the recovery further into the future.

On the positive end, CLF management did make some positive comments during the earnings call which could provide some a boost to the share price in the coming months.

Firstly, they expect that in Q2 the company's bottom line would benefit both from higher pricing and higher shipments which would continue to be tailwinds in the following quarters as well.

Secondly, recent geopolitical events are destabilizing steel supply chains, while higher energy costs favor locally-produced steel due to transport costs. This should put CLF in a very good position to take advantage of its high exposure to auto OEMs, which prioritize safety of steel deliveries.

We already see some evidence of higher demand in CLF's cash flow statement where cash outflows due to changes in account receivables have nearly doubled from the same quarter a year ago.

As a result, CLF share price could potentially reverse its downward course in the second half of 2026, provided that macroeconomic conditions do not deteriorate further.

However, investors should also be prepared for high share price volatility as both profitability and valuation gaps between CLF and its two major peers - Steel Dynamics (STLD) and Nucor (NUE), remain very large and that makes it much harder for the market to put a fair price on the company in the short-term.

Investor Takeaway

CLF share price is down sharply after a report that was roughly in-line with expectations. The market reaction, however, could hardly be classified as an overreaction given the company's struggle to achieve gross profitability after steel tariffs took place. Share price volatility would most likely remain elevated through the rest of 2026 with CLF now being in a good position to deliver solid Q2 report, if macroeconomic conditions do not deteriorate in the meantime.

data.title: Realty Income: Still A Go-To REIT For Income Investors
data.description:
Introduction

Realty Income (O) owns a diversified portfolio of real estate across the US and Europe, with strong fundamentals and an attractive monthly dividend yield, expanding significantly despite playing in an uncertain macro environment.

Despite the ongoing uncertainty and potential wave of inflation caused by the Iran conflict, Realty Income’s foundation remains strong, while the dividend yield continues to be attractive and well-covered, still offering a good level to add at.

Fundamentals Remain Strong

Realty Income reported a solid year given the ongoing macro pressure, with an AFFO of ~$3.89 billion ($4.28 per share), meaning a decent 2.15% increase YoY on a per share basis, while investing a solid $6.3 billion domestically and especially internationally, expanding their footprint in Europe at a combined initial weighted average cash yield of 7.3%.

Overall, the company continues to be in a strong position, with a very solid 8.8-year weighted average lease term and a diversified portfolio of tenants across the US and Europe, boasting an A3/A- (Stable) rated balance sheet at Moody’s and S&P Global.

For 2026, Realty Income expects a solid AFFO per share of $4.38 to $4.42, representing an increase of 2.8% at midpoint, which is not bad given the current environment, expecting same-store rent growth of only 1% to 1.3% alongside an occupancy rate of 98.5%, while expecting their investment volume to ramp up to a whopping $8 billion in 2026. At midpoint, given the current number of shares, we get to an AFFO of $4.1 billion in 2026.

Financially, based on Realty Income’s latest report, we see an overall solid position for a company like this, with a manageable level of debt, a 5.4x Net Debt to Annualized Pro Forma Adj. EBITDAre ratio as of Q4 and a solid ~$434.8 million worth of cash and cash equivalents available.

Meanwhile, the debt is spread well into the future, with no significant debt walls to worry about, although the high-rate environment is a risk for refinancing, at the end of the day. Still, their recent $800 million 2033 notes come at a 4.75% coupon, which is still solid in this environment, but thanks to their ability to tap into the European market, they executed a $500 million dollar-to-euro cross-currency swap that brings the blended coupon to 4.16% instead, which is yet another advantage of their European expansion, as I’ll mention below.

Regarding the dividend, this remains attractive despite a recent increase in their stock price, currently paying a ~5.02% yield, distributed monthly (of course, as this is “The Monthly Dividend Company®” after all), although they announced a relatively weak 0.2% increase not long ago. This represents a very solid payout ratio of only ~75.84% based on 2025’s AFFO per share, allowing the company to continue hiking it for longer even with lower growth rates if they want to.

O will release their Q1 report on May 6, with the market expecting Normalized FFO of $1.10 and $1.39 billion in revenue. Although I don’t expect some major changes or swings, I expect their tone to be careful overall, as the environment warrants it given the ongoing uncertainty surrounding the potential Iran-driven inflation wave, with the company potentially giving insights into how the market is reacting to it at a more in-depth level.

Iran Pressure Can Last For Longer

Right now, the market’s attention is certainly on the Iran conflict, as that has very significant and clearly visible effects on the prices of vital commodities that fuel the global economy, which can rapidly deteriorate as a result, potentially hurting O, their tenants, and the final consumer alongside their valuation.

Although the latest development is the recent ceasefire news, it’s important to remember that not only did we not reach a “complete” peace yet (and that in itself is questionable in the long term), but the environment is also likely at a point of no return in terms of the inflationary wave that can come out of it, since the region’s energy infrastructure has been heavily damaged and can take months or even years to bring back to previous flows. This means that war premiums are likely to persist, especially if the demand jumps as a result of companies or even countries rushing in to rebuild reserves.

It may not look like something that important for a real estate landlord, but not only are we talking about vital parts of the economy that can slow down their tenants, but also something that can lead to higher interest rates or, at the very least, a higher-for-longer environment, with several institutions already cutting their growth outlooks for the year and warning of a potential recession if things don’t de-escalate fast—all while the consumer environment is already weak.

This is also why the Fed decided to keep rates steady back in March, warning of the potential inflationary pressure coming from the conflict in the Middle East and projecting a single cut in 2026, although there are now growing concerns of no cuts at all during the year.

Also, let’s not forget what caused this weakness in REITs in the first place, as the high-rate environment affects these companies significantly from several points of view—which I believe is very important to understand about this asset class and has been responsible for a lot of negativity over the past few years given their natural underperformance.

Not only are borrowing costs for them and their tenants higher (risk of more expensive refinancing, slowdowns, fewer investments, etc.), but with treasury yields so high now (10-year notes jumping above 4.4% not long ago before retreating a bit), the market has to take into account if the risk these assets come with is worth paying, generally resulting in either lower prices or similar ones to the levels from 5-6 years ago for most REITs, with O still well below the >$80 levels seen in February 2020.

Europe Unlocks Long-Term Potential

There are some positives too, however, as the economy has been showing signs of a potential recovery before this conflict, and if it ends soon, the shock can likely be absorbed despite some near-term pressure, while the supply of new construction in several areas of real estate remains constrained as a result of the environment, potentially allowing O to capitalize on their solid portfolio and deliver better lease spreads or even better occupancy rates if it happens alongside a recovery in the demand.

Meanwhile, Realty Income has the advantage of a solid and growing footprint in Europe, which sets them apart from most other peers and offers the potential for decades of growth and also access to better yields as a result of the lack of significant competition in their area combined with better local debt costs, which they can reinvest and deliver competitive returns.

Another example besides the one mentioned above would be the €1.3 billion euro-denominated senior unsecured notes issued back in June at an average tenor of ~8 years and a weighted average yield to maturity of only 3.693%, which is significantly better than US debt, while the cap rates are similar given that they invested ~$3.71 billion in Europe at an average initial cash yield of 7.4% compared to 7.3% in the US.

Not only are we talking about better borrowing costs, but the initial weighted-average cash yields are actually higher, so it’s no wonder why Realty Income invested more in Europe than in the US in 2025, which is something I highlighted for W. P. Carey (WPC) during their latest coverage, which also talked about not intersecting too much with Realty Income thanks to its size.

Risks Reiterated

Ultimately, regardless of how solid or even resilient the company is and the advantages of their international expansion, the stock is far from immune to market fear, as we’ve seen over and over again in the past.

This can translate into more pressure on the stock in the near term given the impact of the Iran conflict on inflation and potentially on the Fed’s/ECB’s next moves, which can lead to more volatility and better prices.

Valuation

Starting from Realty Income’s midpoint AFFO guidance of $4.1 billion in 2026 estimated above, I’ll assume a 4.5% CAGR over the first 5 years, possible thanks to their rent escalators, potential improvements in the environment, and especially their aggressive expansion in Europe or partnerships such as the one with Apollo Global Management (APO), with a 3.5% CAGR for the subsequent 5 years, a 2.5% terminal growth rate, and an elevated discount rate of 10%, which I believe is conservative for a company like this given the macro risks and their solid fundamentals, with 932.44 million shares outstanding as of February 20, 2026, when they filed the latest earnings report.

This gets us to an equity value of $60.15 billion, for an intrinsic value of $64.51 per share, which is nearly identical to the current $65.09, although it’s important to note that I’m assuming a high discount rate of 10%, while a more “normal” 8.5% in a good environment gets us to $81.05, which is very similar to the levels from before the pandemic.

Conclusions

Realty Income is a Buy, backed by a solid foundation, attractive and sustainable yield, and long-term potential, especially from their expansion in Europe, being a solid contender for a long-term portfolio.

There are risks given the ongoing Iran pressure and macro uncertainty, but the company’s size and borrowing costs can also allow them to take advantage of such an environment, while more fear can unlock an even better opportunity.

data.title: Since 1953 This Is How The S&P 500 Has Performed After A Major Geopolitical Shock
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I reiterate my buy recommendation on assets that track the main American indices. This article is part of a weekly series starting on November 7, 2024, where I bring valuable insights to SA readers. Amid contradictory news regarding the War in Iran, my intention in this article is to show the size of the opportunity generated by the situation.

Context

Incredible as it may seem, the S&P 500 has already recovered from the drop after the War in Iran. Since the start of the War on February 28th until last Friday, the S&P 500 has already risen more than 3.5%, throwing cold water on alarmist analysts and skeptical investors. I already expected such behavior, but not with such force.

The last week was the third in a row in which the S&P 500 rose above 3%. This performance only occurred in June 2020 in the recovery after the fall of C-19 and also in September 1982 and September 1940.

The turnaround in the rally was the 2nd fastest in history since 1950 and was only second to 1982, when the FED was under the command of Paul Volcker.

The data shows how strong the market is, but as I said, I already expected this behavior. Financial market specialists have analyzed the performance of the S&P 500 following major geopolitical events since the Korean War in 1953. The result is an average increase of 14.2% after 12 months. Of course you can argue that it is not possible to compare the current economy with the economy at the time of other geopolitical events, but I have solid arguments to believe that a similar performance will occur again.

Bullish Arguments

The next earnings season for the S&P 500 is full of expectations. The market has not expected profits to grow above 15% year on year for a long time, and as we know, companies have beaten market estimates in recent quarters.

I am also extremely optimistic about the valuation. The drop in shares of software companies combined with the War in Iran caused the valuation of the tech sector to fall from 40x earnings to 20x earnings. For a long time I have seen investors skeptical due to the expensive valuation; well, this seems like an opportunity to buy the shares at a fairer price.

Finally, the data indicates that insiders of the technology companies represented by XLK are buying shares at the highest level in the last 15 years. Without a doubt, they are taking advantage of the attractive valuation to make good investments.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. My target for the S&P 500 is 7778 points by the end of 2026, and I am optimistic about the recommendation when I see that SA's powerful Quant tools also indicate buy.

Before addressing the risks, I remind you that I offer my recommended stock portfolio. The portfolio has a mix of American stocks and Brazilian dividend-paying stocks, as I explain in detail in another article. Year-to-date the portfolio rises 9.8% while the S&P 500 rises 4.1%.

Potential Threats To The Thesis

It is possible to deduce that investors would leave fear behind and become optimistic after the market recovery, and that is exactly what happened, as the research below shows. However, I will continue this series weekly, alerting investors of opportunities even when the market falls.

It is interesting that despite the drop in shares and contraction in the valuation of tech companies, some metrics still have expensive valuations, especially when compared to wider windows, for example, 2010.

Finally, the news is contradictory regarding the complete resolution of the conflict, which brings more uncertainty to the thesis. Not to mention that the conflict greatly reduced expectations of interest rate cuts due to high inflation. At the moment the market is only pricing in a cut in interest rates.

The Bottom Line

The American stock market has completely recovered from the fall after the Iran War. Although I expected this performance, the strength of the movement surprised me. Only a few times in history has the market shown so much strength to recover.

Once again, investors have an example of the importance of buying in times of fear and panic. Despite all the negative headlines, company profits continue to rise, valuations have become more attractive, and even insiders indicate that it is time to buy.

Based on this analysis, I reiterate the recommendation to buy assets that track the main American indices. The investor should not fear the contradictions in the news regarding the end of the conflict but rather take advantage of it to make an attractive investment.

data.title: This Rally Has A Trap Door
data.description:
Thesis Summary

Over the last 10 days, stocks have staged an absolutely historic rally.

With encouraging news that the war may be over, oil coming off the highs, and AI stocks continuing to deliver, it seems like sentiment has shifted quickly.

I recently talked about the key market forces that were helping markets rally. Things like options, positioning, and liquidity tailwinds.

This creates a reflexive scenario, where higher prices force market participants to chase and create a reflexive loop.

But reflexivity works both ways.

If the narrative begins to shift, then a historic rally could be followed by a historic sell-off.

Renewed stress in Hormuz, an AI slowdown, stubborn inflation, and political instability are all possible triggers that could force a reflexive loop, only this time to the downside.

Is Hormuz Really Open?

If you believe some of the headlines from last week, then you’d be inclined to think the Strait of Hormuz is a non-issue. But at the very least, there are some mixed opinions.

As I write this, Iran has said it has no intentions to continue talks with the U.S. following an embargo of an Iranian ship. Moreover, it seems like Iran is ready to retaliate.

I maintain what I said in a previous article. I think oil may have stopped, or at least it won't go much higher from here.

But if we keep at over $100 levels for the next few months, this is going to become a very real drag on the economy.

The market seems highly complacent here, and this gives us a clear downside set-up.

AI Slowdown

If geopolitics is the obvious risk, AI is a more subtle one that we can’t overlook.

AI has become the backbone of this market rally and the economy. It’s the spending from hyperscalers that is really helping maintain high corporate earnings, paired with AI efficiencies.

This is all well and good until it isn't.

It doesn’t need to collapse in order for sentiment to shift.

A delay in deployments, a moderation in capex growth, or weaker-than-expected monetization can be enough.

Any evidence of this?

Nvidia's (NVDA) Rubin ramp is reportedly facing delays. Furthermore, this analysis contends 40% of data centers are facing delays.

All of this, while AI valuations and positioning are at all-time highs.

AI is still a powerful long-term trend. But in the short term, it has become crowded, consensus, and heavily owned. That combination rarely ends quietly.

Inflation Pick Up

This is one I’ve gone back and forth on.

Before the war, it was clear that inflation looked to be cooling, but this has quickly reversed.

The most recent Goldman Sachs (GS) forecasts show potential for PCE to head beyond 3%, and that’s the baseline.

It could get much worse depending on how long the war continues. The next few inflation prints could be something the market is not pricing in just yet.

Political Instability And Seasonality

Last, and certainly not least, this is historically the worst time to be invested in the market.

Based on an analysis of the 4-year Presidential Cycle, the months leading up to midterms are some of the worst.

This is likely due to increased instability and uncertainty, and the picture right now certainly looks quite volatile, with President Donald Trump at an all-time low.

Reflexivity Works Both Ways

In my last market piece, I highlighted the key reasons why the market was rallying hard.

This included CTAs chasing equities, as they were underallocated, as well as excessive short positioning, creating a short squeeze.

Now, however, we're in danger of seeing the opposite happen.

Investors have piled back into the long side, as reflected by the put-to-call ratio. This doesn’t mean a pullback is due by itself, but it’s a possible indicator.

Let’s pair this with the fact that SPX is back at overbought levels on the daily RSI.

At the very least, we should see markets consolidate.

Lastly, it’s quite telling that the On Balance Volume is far from its recent highs. This often precedes a larger sell-off to the downside and supports our thesis.

This is a reflective rally, based on a quick shift in positioning and options flows.

It’s not backed up in fundamentals, and a change in sentiment will send it back to the lows as fast as we made these highs.

Final Thoughts

The market has shown impressive resilience, but now’s the time to be cautious.

Right now, investors are treating a range of risks as manageable, but this complacency could easily backfire.

If things go right, there may still be upside, but if things go wrong, the downside could be amplified by the very dynamics that drove the rally in the first place.

Reflexivity works both ways.

data.title: The Credit Market Lens: Software Stuck In A Trough
data.description:
The software sector remains challenged by pressured valuations, uncertain recoveries, and less reliable sponsor support.

The software sector remains stuck in a trough across the capital structure. Software equities saw a meaningful relief rally last week but remain down about 20% year-to-date, while the modest March recovery in software leveraged loans has stalled (see Figure 1). Despite compositional differences – public equities generally represent larger companies with more scale, liquidity, and financial flexibility than the typically smaller, private-equity-owned issuers that dominate the software loan market – the outcome is the same: Neither market has been able to fully retrace the year-to-date sell-off in a meaningful way.

Figure 1: Software sector equities and loans remain near recent lows

Public software equities are effectively long-duration assets (they typically pay no dividend and rely more on long-term cash flow expectations than other sectors), so the March backup in interest rates was not particularly helpful.

In credit, investors have historically leaned on the “sponsor put” – i.e., the ability of financial sponsors to support portfolio companies via add-on capital, debt buybacks, and other liability management tools – but that backstop has been less reliable this time.

The good news is that relatively benign near-term refinancing needs for software companies limit the risk of an abrupt rise in financial distress (see Figure 2). That said, this is hardly a clean bill of health, and the fundamental challenges around terminal values are unlikely to dissipate anytime soon. An inflection in the broader business cycle would almost certainly turn latent, sector-specific vulnerabilities into higher defaults over time.

Figure 2: Debt maturity walls for software borrowers in the leveraged loan and direct lending markets appear benign

If a software-led default cycle were to emerge, however, history offers limited direct guidance. The software sector as we know it today, characterized by loan-heavy capital structures and significant sponsor involvement, has not experienced a meaningful episode of financial distress. As a result, empirical evidence on default intensity and recovery behavior remains scant.

That does not mean history is silent. There are precedents of industry-specific shocks driven by technological change that resulted in asset obsolescence or excess supply. The rise of the internet and the ensuing print media defaults of the early 2000s illustrate the former, while shale production and the resulting energy sector distress exemplify the latter.

These episodes suggest a common pattern: When depressed residual asset values coincide with a concentration of distressed supply, recovery rates tend to be materially lower than average. Consistent with this, Figure 3 shows that recoveries during such periods, particularly in recessions, are notably lower than in low-default environments.

Figure 3: Recovery rates tend to be lower when distressed supply is high, such as in recessions

Another look at dispersion in BDC portfolios

Valuation opacity in corporate direct lending portfolios remains front and center. Investors increasingly recognize that the smoother return profile over the past decade for this subset of private markets largely reflects lower observed volatility, not inherently lower risk. What is less appreciated is the extent to which opaque valuation practices and stale price marks have distorted the cross-sectional distribution of loan prices within BDC portfolios.

These practices have artificially compressed dispersion and created an appearance of stability that is not fully consistent with underlying credit diversity. This stands in sharp contrast to the broadly syndicated loan market, where continuous price discovery produces a wider, more informative range of outcomes across outstanding loans.

Two metrics underscore this gap. First, for loans held across multiple BDCs, the spread between the most conservative and most optimistic marks has widened meaningfully in recent quarters (see Figure 4). This divergence signals growing uncertainty around true valuations and has contributed to recent redemption pressures.

Figure 4: The spread between the most conservative and most optimistic marks in BDC portfolios finished 2025 at a new high

Second, since 2021, the overall price dispersion within BDC portfolios has been an order of magnitude lower than in the broadly syndicated loan market, leading to an implausibly tight range given broadly comparable credit risk (see Figure 5). In a stress scenario in which marks are forced to converge toward realizable value, dispersion would likely widen abruptly.

Figure 5: BDC portfolios show a much lower price dispersion than syndicated loans in recent years

Taken together, the software credit landscape looks stable on the surface but more fragile underneath. This argues for selectivity, a focus on downside mitigation, and caution toward strategies that rely on smooth reported valuations rather than resilient fundamentals.

Disclosures

Statements concerning financial market trends or portfolio strategies are based on current market conditions, which will fluctuate. There is no guarantee that these investment strategies will work under all market conditions or are appropriate for all investors and each investor should evaluate their ability to invest for the long term, especially during periods of downturn in the market. Outlook and strategies are subject to change without notice.

Charts are provided for illustrative purposes and are not indicative of the past or future performance of any PIMCO product. Forecasts, estimates and certain information contained herein are based upon proprietary research and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. There is no guarantee that results will be achieved.

Past performance is not a guarantee or a reliable indicator of future results. References to specific securities and their issuers are not intended and should not be interpreted as recommendations to purchase, sell or hold such securities. PIMCO products and strategies may or may not include the securities referenced and, if such securities are included, no representation is being made that such securities will continue to be included.

All Investments contain risk and may lose value. An investment in a BDC is subject to credit and investment risk, leverage risk, market and valuation risk, price volatility risk, liquidity risk, interest rate risk and structural and regulatory risk. Private credit involves an investment in non-publicly traded securities which may be subject to illiquidity risk. Portfolios that invest in private credit may be leveraged and may engage in speculative investment practices that increase the risk of investment loss.

PIMCO as a general matter provides services to qualified institutions, financial intermediaries and institutional investors. Individual investors should contact their own financial professional to determine the most appropriate investment options for their financial situation. This material contains the opinions of the author and such opinions are subject to change without notice. This material has been distributed for informational purposes only and should not be considered as investment advice or a recommendation of any particular security, strategy or investment product. Information contained herein has been obtained from sources believed to be reliable, but not guaranteed. No part of this material may be reproduced in any form, or referred to in any other publication, without express written permission. PIMCO is a trademark of Allianz Asset Management of America LLC in the United States and throughout the world.
